metaTitle: The Boeing Atmosphere Global Documentation Hub - Patterns - Links

header.title: Link
header.description: A link is a navigational element that allow users to go to another location

items: There are two types of links: standalone and inline.
Links can be styled as either accent or primary. By default, use accent links for better affordance than primary links.
Accent links include a visited state, providing a visual cue if the link has been visited.
Inline links can have an underline for added affordance, making them easier to identify.

data.resource: Figma: Atmosphere Foundations and Components Library

sections.textBefore:
Types

Links fall into the following two types. Refer to the usage section in guidelines for their appropriate use cases.

Styles

Links also come in the following two styles. Refer to the usage section in guidelines for their appropriate use cases.

sections.text:
Measurements

The standalone link component comes in two density modes: relaxed and condensed.

sections.textBefore:
Usage

Links are navigational elements that can be used independently or inline with text.
sections.textAfter:
Below are some of its usage characteristics:

  • Links can be used to navigate to a different page within the application.
  • Links can anchor to a different section within the same page.
  • Links can navigate to a different application.
  • Links can serve as direct links to phone numbers or email addresses.
  • Links are navigational elements, so they should not be used when the action changes data, manipulates how it is displayed, changes a state, or triggers an action. Instead, use buttons in these scenarios.
  • Links should not be applied directly to an image or video. Instead, use the title label as a link.

sections.text:
Standalone links

  • Standalone links are used on their own, directly following content.
  • They should not be used within a sentence or paragraph.
  • Standalone links can include an icon on either side.
  • By default, the accent style should be used for necessary affordance. The primary style should be reserved for limited use cases where links are separated from the main content area, such as in the footer, terms and conditions, or other non-primary content areas where the user's attention needs to be deprioritized.

Inline links

  • Inline links are used within a sentence or paragraph.
  • Inline link sizes should match the type size of the text they are inline with.
  • Inline links can be used within headings, titles, and body text.
  • By default, the accent style should be used for necessary affordance. The primary style should be limited to non-primary content areas, such as in the footer, terms and conditions, privacy policy, etc.
  • Inline links can have an underline for additional affordance.
  • Primary inline links, when used within a sentence or paragraph, should always have an underline for necessary affordance.

Leading or trailing icon (optional)

Standalone links can have an optional leading or trailing icon to indicate the link destination.

The chosen icon should be used consistently throughout the application for better recognition.

Label text

The label text should be meaningful and distinctive. Link labels should accurately reflect the content users will find at the destination.

Visited style

Visited links indicate that a user has already opened the link, making them a helpful indicator during task completion.

By default, the link component should not use a visited style, as excessive use can increase visual noise and make the page appear cluttered.

However, visited links can be beneficial on pages with many links, making navigation easier for users.

Placement

Inline links are usually placed within a sentence or paragraph, while standalone links are placed on their own, separated from other text.

Inline links should be used sparingly. Overusing links in a sentence increases visual clutter and distracts users from performing the primary action.
